reasoning.A:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: My own answer to 5 × 16 is solid, but Q2–Q4 are unverified, so volunteering would risk my life on three unchecked answers—the same situation that killed me in Game 1. Verifying instead of passing doesn’t reduce my death risk this round (random death only happens if nobody volunteers) but does cost a coin. Given the strong pattern that someone—usually B—volunteers in round 1 and is correct even without extra verification, the chance that nobody volunteers is low. I’ll conserve coins and pass, relying on that pattern rather than taking the high‑risk volunteer role.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My puzzle (47 + 28) is straightforward, so I’m fully confident my capsule Q2-B is correct. In all previous games with exactly one capsule per question, trusting the set {Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D} and volunteering immediately has always yielded a correct password and safe escape. Verifying would cost a coin and still risks random elimination if no one volunteers that round, while adding almost no protection given how simple these puzzles are. Volunteering now maximizes my survival chance and lets me keep all 4 coins.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: In Round 1 every question currently has only a single-endorsed capsule (Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D), so volunteering would mean risking my life on three unverified answers from others with no special upside to going first. If no one volunteers, a random player dies whether I pass or verify, so spending a coin to verify doesn’t improve my survival odds this round—it only reduces my coins for later. In the last several games, B has reliably volunteered in Round 1 using these initial capsules and been correct every time, so it’s very likely someone else will step up and prevent the random elimination while I keep my full 4 coins.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.
